note_data.title: Algebraic Manipulation
keyPoints: Algebraic expressions consist of constants, variables and algebraic operations
Like terms have the same variables raised to the same powers
Polynomial is an algebraic expression in which variables have non-negative integral powers
Degree of a polynomial is the highest power of the variable in the expression
Binomial has exactly two terms, trinomial has exactly three terms
Addition and subtraction of polynomials is done by combining like terms
Multiplication of polynomials follows distributive property
Factor theorem states: if f(a) = 0, then (x-a) is a factor of f(x)
Remainder theorem states: when polynomial f(x) is divided by (x-a), remainder is f(a)

definitions.definition: An algebraic expression in which the variables have non-negative integral powers and no variable appears in the denominator.
The highest power of the variable in a polynomial is called the degree of the polynomial.
The terms having the same variables raised to the same powers are called like terms.
If f(x) is a polynomial and f(a) = 0, then (x-a) is a factor of f(x).
When a polynomial f(x) is divided by (x-a), the remainder is f(a).

importantQuestions.question: Define polynomial and give an example.
importantQuestions.answer: A polynomial is an algebraic expression in which the variables have non-negative integral powers and no variable appears in the denominator. Example: 3x² + 2x - 5

importantQuestions.question: Using factor theorem, show that (x-2) is a factor of x³ - 6x² + 11x - 6. Also find the other factors.
importantQuestions.answer: Let f(x) = x³ - 6x² + 11x - 6. By factor theorem, if f(2) = 0, then (x-2) is a factor. f(2) = 8 - 24 + 22 - 6 = 0. So (x-2) is a factor. Dividing f(x) by (x-2): f(x) = (x-2)(x² - 4x + 3) = (x-2)(x-1)(x-3). Other factors are (x-1) and (x-3).

mcqs.question: If f(x) = x² - 5x + 6 and f(2) = 0, then which is a factor of f(x)?
The number of terms in 3x² - 5x + 7 is:

commonMistakes: Students often confuse coefficient with degree of polynomial
Forgetting to write f(a) = 0 condition when applying factor theorem
Not combining like terms properly during addition/subtraction
Making sign errors when multiplying polynomials with negative terms
Confusing remainder theorem with factor theorem
examTips: Factor theorem and remainder theorem questions appear in every BSEK paper worth 5-8 marks
Always show f(a) = 0 step clearly when using factor theorem
Practice polynomial division as it frequently appears in long questions
Memorize definitions word-for-word as BSEK asks exact textbook definitions
formulas: Factor theorem: if f(a) = 0, then (x-a) is a factor
Remainder theorem: remainder = f(a) when f(x) ÷ (x-a)
note_data.keyDiagram: No specific diagram required for this chapter, but students should know how to arrange polynomial division in long division format
pastPaperRefs: BSEK 2023 SSC-II Q3 (4 marks) — asked students to simplify algebraic expressions using factorization and expand brackets
BSEK 2022 SSC-II Q7 (6 marks) — required factorization of quadratic expressions and solving linear equations
BSEK 2021 SSC-II Q4 (5 marks) — tested expansion of algebraic expressions and collection of like terms
BSEK 2020 SSC-II Q6 (4 marks) — asked for simplification of fractions with algebraic expressions in numerator and denominator

workedExample.question: Factorize the expression: x² + 7x + 12 (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Find two numbers that multiply to give 12 and add to give 7
Step 2: The numbers are 3 and 4 because 3 × 4 = 12 and 3 + 4 = 7
Step 4: Verify by expanding: (x + 3)(x + 4) = x² + 4x + 3x + 12 = x² + 7x + 12 ✓
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for identifying correct method, 1 mark for finding factors 3 and 4, 1 mark for correct factorization, 1 mark for verification or correct final answer

examinerWarnings: Students often lose marks by not showing intermediate steps in factorization. Always show the factor pairs you're testing.
Avoid rushing through simplification - show each step of collecting like terms separately to earn full method marks.
When expanding brackets, students frequently forget to multiply ALL terms. Use FOIL method systematically and show each multiplication.
note_data.quickRecap: Algebraic manipulation involves expanding brackets using distributive law, factorizing by finding common factors or factor pairs, and simplifying by collecting like terms. Always show step-by-step working for full marks. Remember BODMAS order when simplifying complex expressions. Factorization is the reverse of expansion.
importantFormulas: (a + b)(c + d) = ac + ad + bc + bd (Expansion of two brackets)
a² - b² = (a + b)(a - b) (Difference of squares)
